Rationale: Highest direct value: it turns completed edits into published videos on the first-party platform, which is the clearest path to user value and business impact.
A clean training set is a prerequisite for every downstream ML workflow, so it deserves early priority despite being invisible to end users.
Annotation setup is a foundational dependency for supervised model quality and has strong execution confidence with manageable effort.
Caption generation removes a major creator bottleneck and improves both accessibility and video quality for most users most of the time.

Feature: Create app to edit the clips and text
Rationale: The editing surface is essential because creators need to review, correct, and finalize AI outputs before publishing.

column11: Metadata generation cuts posting friction and improves discoverability while still allowing the creator to edit outputs.

Feature: Prepare data: Split videos into clips
Rationale: Clip-level segmentation data is required to train the most important automation in the MVP: turning raw cooking footage into recipe steps.
High-quality semantic labels are required for meaningful AI assistance in captions, recipes, and later classification workflows.

Feature: Build ML model to clip video into individual recipe steps
Rationale: This is the core automation leap in the product. Confidence is moderate because of model difficulty, but it is too central to exclude from MVP.
Important risk-reduction work, but excluded from the formal MVP list to stay within the assignment cap. In practice it should still happen before launch.
Also critical for launch quality, but excluded from the formal MVP feature count to preserve a ruthless, cap-compliant scope.

Feature: Build ML model to classify recipe steps
Rationale: Helpful enhancement, but the MVP can still provide strong value with segmentation, captions, metadata generation, and manual editing controls.
Cross-platform publishing has value, but it is not required to prove the core TastyTube creation loop in the first release.
Deferred to reduce launch complexity and keep the team focused on the first-party publishing workflow.
Same rationale as other external publishing targets: useful, but not essential to the MVP value path.
A good onboarding aid, but secondary to getting the core creation workflow working reliably.
Strategically attractive, but too costly and too dependent on high-quality source outputs for the first launch.
Platform extensibility is a long-term investment rather than a first-release necessity.
